explain: The sequence depicts cortical connections strengthening over time while the memory becomes less dependent on hippocampal links. That is the large-scale stabilization described as system consolidation.
hint: Follow where the durable connections are located in the final panel.

q: What two properties characterize hippocampal replay as presented in the chapter?
options: It repeats an encoded activity sequence faster while preserving temporal order.
It reverses every activity sequence and stretches it over a much longer interval.
It removes all cortical activity so the hippocampus can permanently store the event alone.
It reproduces only semantic facts and excludes spatial or episodic sequences.
explain: Hippocampal replay repeats a sequence of neural activity after initial encoding. The replay is typically compressed in time but retains the original temporal order, and some researchers view it as essential to system consolidation.
hint: The same ordered pattern returns, but on a faster clock.

q: In the place-cell figure, an ordered firing sequence unfolds across about six seconds while a rat is active and reappears across about two-tenths of a second while it sleeps. What is the best interpretation?
options: Sleep erased the active sequence and replaced it with unrelated cell firing.
The active sequence was replayed during sleep in a temporally compressed form.
The sleeping sequence proves that temporal order is irrelevant to consolidation.
The rat converted spatial procedural memory into a consciously declared fact.
explain: The same ordered place-cell pattern appears during sleep on a much shorter timescale. This is the figure's example of faster hippocampal replay that preserves sequence order after the original experience.
hint: Compare both the order of colored cells and the time axes.

q: Why might a car-accident victim forget events from the hours before the crash while retaining much older memories?
options: Recent events were stored only as implicit motor programs, whereas old events were semantic from birth.
The crash selectively removes the primacy effect but leaves the recency effect completely intact.
Older memories had more time for slow system consolidation and were therefore more stable.
Recent events had already moved permanently to cortex, whereas old memories remained fragile in STM.
explain: System consolidation is much slower than synaptic consolidation and can continue across very long periods. Older memories have had more opportunity to stabilize, while events from shortly before trauma may still be vulnerable.
hint: Compare how much consolidation time recent and remote memories had before the accident.

q: According to the chapter summary, what is transfer learning in artificial neural networks?
options: A new network discards all earlier learning so it can model an unrelated task from scratch.
A network converts short-term activations into permanent biological synapses in the hippocampus.
An existing network memorizes every training item and transfers them unchanged to human memory.
An older network helps train a new one on a task with shared information.
explain: The summary describes transfer learning as using an older, existing network to train a new network on a task that shares common information. Reusing relevant prior learning can make training quicker and produce better modeling.
hint: The new task benefits from information already learned by an existing network.

q: Which description best matches autobiographical memory?
options: Episodic events from one's life plus semantic facts about oneself
Only vivid emotional events shared by an entire culture and preserved without later change
General world knowledge that contains no personal information
Motor skills performed automatically without conscious recall
explain: Autobiographical memory combines episodic memories of personally experienced events with semantic facts about the self. Flashbulb memories are only one emotional subset, general world knowledge is not necessarily autobiographical, and motor skills are procedural memory.
hint: Think about both personal events and personal facts.

q: Why are retrospective interviews a limited way to study autobiographical memory?
options: They can measure only implicit memories and not explicit reports
Researchers lack control over encoding order and cannot guarantee accuracy
They prevent participants from describing sensory or emotional components
Researchers must recreate each remembered event under controlled conditions during the interview
explain: Autobiographical events occurred outside the laboratory, so researchers cannot control how or when they were encoded and often cannot independently determine whether a report is accurate. Interviews can include explicit, sensory, and emotional details; the researcher does not create the original event.
hint: Ask what an experimenter loses when the event happened long before the study.

q: The speaker notes describe autobiographical remembering as mental time travel with spatial, emotional, and sensory components. What does this emphasize?
options: Autobiographical memories contain only factual self-knowledge
Personal memories are stored as isolated verbal labels
Autobiographical remembering can reconstruct several dimensions of an event
All personal memories preserve a complete multisensory recording of the original experience
explain: The speaker notes call mental time travel multidimensional: a remembered event may reconstruct where it occurred, how it felt, and what was sensed. That does not make the memory a complete or perfectly accurate recording.
hint: Focus on what it means for one memory to contain several kinds of detail.

q: After remembering how a previous all-night study session hurt her performance, Mina starts reviewing earlier for the next exam. Which function of autobiographical memory is illustrated?
options: Social, because the memory creates a bond
Self-representational, because it protects a stable identity
Semantic, because it removes all event-specific information
Directive, because a past event guides a current action
explain: The directive function uses retrospection to guide present behavior. The example does not center on sharing an experience, maintaining identity, or losing episodic detail.
hint: Which function turns a remembered event into a decision rule?

q: Two new classmates become closer after exchanging stories about difficult first days at school. Which autobiographical-memory function is most direct?
options: Social, because sharing experiences can create bonds
Directive, because each person is planning a future action
Self-representational, because each story fixes a permanent identity
Reconsolidation, because both original memories must be replaced
explain: The social function allows people to share experiences and build social bonds. The scenario emphasizes connection rather than action guidance, identity consistency, or memory replacement.
hint: Look at what changes between the two people after they tell the stories.

q: A person recalls years of volunteering when deciding, 'Helping others is the kind of person I am.' Which function is operating?
options: Directive, because the memory supplies a procedure for every future volunteering decision
Self-representational, because the past supports a stable self-image
Social, because the person is sharing the memory with a group
Infantile, because the memory comes from an early life period
explain: The self-representational function uses the past to support a consistent self-image and stable identity. No social exchange, action procedure, or early-childhood forgetting is central to the example.
hint: The key clue is the claim about who the person is.

q: In the self-memory system, how do the autobiographical knowledge base and working self differ?
options: The knowledge base stores current goals; the working self permanently stores every detailed life event
The knowledge base contains only episodic details; the working self contains only facts
The knowledge base stores past-life information; the working self uses current goals to access it
The knowledge base is conscious; the working self is permanently inaccessible
explain: The autobiographical knowledge base is the store of information and facts about past life events. The working self is the active set of current personal goals that determines how that store is accessed.
hint: Match 'cold storage' with the active workspace.

q: A student has many memories of high school, but while preparing a scholarship essay, memories related to persistence become especially accessible. The self-memory system attributes this selection mainly to the:
options: automatic conversion of all semantic facts into episodes
fixed encoding order that automatically makes scholarship-relevant high-school events accessible
loss of every memory unrelated to academic achievement
working self using a current goal to search the knowledge base
explain: The working self contains current goals and controls access to the autobiographical knowledge base. The scholarship goal makes relevant memories easier to retrieve without changing their original order or deleting unrelated memories.
hint: Which SMS component changes with the person's goal right now?

q: Which sequence moves from the broadest to the most detailed tier of autobiographical memory?
options: Lifetime period → general events → event-specific knowledge
General events → event-specific knowledge → lifetime period
Event-specific knowledge → lifetime period → general events
Working self → lifetime period → autobiographical knowledge base
explain: A lifetime period is broad, such as grade school or a first job. General events are related sequences within that period, and event-specific knowledge supplies the most detailed information.
hint: Begin with an entire era of life and end with the details of one event.

q: Years after a semester abroad, Luis remembers that he lived in Madrid but no longer recalls the sensory details of most individual days. In the SMS framework, this pattern reflects:
options: growth of event-specific knowledge into an increasingly vivid episode with its original sensory context
loss of event-specific knowledge while broader semantic information remains
conversion of a lifetime period into an implicit motor skill
complete preservation of each event in episodic memory
explain: The SMS framework proposes that event-specific knowledge often dissolves, leaving broader general events or lifetime periods. Many episodes therefore fade into semantic self-knowledge or are forgotten.
hint: Notice what survived: the broad fact, not the lived details.

q: Which pattern defines infantile amnesia in the chapter?
options: Adults lose procedural skills learned before adolescence
Children cannot form any explicit or implicit memory until the hippocampus becomes fully mature
Adults recall few or no episodic memories from roughly before ages two to four
Adults remember early events accurately but cannot describe them verbally
explain: Infantile amnesia is the scarcity or absence of adult episodic memories from approximately the first two to four years. A less severe form can affect memories from before age ten, and explicit memory is affected more than implicit memory; young children can still learn.
hint: The definition concerns adult recall of very early episodic events.

q: In Bauer and Larkina's longitudinal work on childhood memory, which group showed the fastest forgetting?
options: Adults recalling emotionally important events from adolescence many decades later
Ten-year-olds recalling recent classroom events
Infants tested only on implicit conditioning
Four-year-olds tracked across later memory tests
explain: Bauer and Larkina tracked memory longitudinally and found that four-year-olds showed the quickest rate of forgetting. The finding helps characterize how autobiographical retention changes across childhood.
hint: Choose the youngest age group explicitly identified in the study result.

q: Which observation most directly supports the hippocampal-change explanation of infantile amnesia?
options: The hippocampus remains underdeveloped until about the age when infantile amnesia ends
Emotional events are always forgotten faster than neutral events throughout childhood and adulthood
Implicit memory disappears at the same rate as explicit memory
Children possess adult-level storytelling ability before age two
explain: The hippocampus supports long-term consolidation and is described as underdeveloped until about age four, roughly when infantile amnesia ends. The other options contradict the chapter's account.
hint: Look for a developmental change whose timing matches the end of the memory gap.

q: How could high neurogenesis contribute to infantile amnesia according to the slides?
options: It prevents children from experiencing or later expressing any emotional reaction to an event
New neurons built during development may overwrite earlier memory traces
It turns every episodic memory into a stable motor habit
New neurons stop the hippocampus from changing after age four
explain: The neurogenesis account proposes that building the developing brain with new neurons may overwrite earlier memories. It does not claim that emotion stops, memories become motor skills, or hippocampal development freezes.
hint: Think about what adding new neural material could do to an existing trace.

q: The language-abilities account of infantile amnesia proposes that early memories are difficult to retrieve later because young children:
options: encode only the emotional tone of an event and none of its spatial or sensory features
have no functioning autobiographical knowledge base
lack the mature language needed to reconstruct an episodic story
use semantic memory only after the age of ten
explain: This account links episodic reconstruction to the language used for storytelling. Juvenile language abilities may make early events difficult to reconstruct later, even though children can still learn and remember in other ways.
hint: The explanation centers on how a remembered event is organized into a narrative.

q: An adult cannot retrieve an early event that was encoded in a very different emotional state, although the event affected later behavior. Which proposed explanation fits best?
options: Hippocampal replay, because the event was repeatedly consolidated into a detailed adult narrative
Self-image formation, because identity peaks before age four
Neurogenesis, because all behavioral learning must have been erased
State-dependent learning, because the internal retrieval state no longer matches
explain: The state-dependent account suggests that young children react to events in ways unlike their later selves, making old memories difficult to retrieve when the internal state differs. The preserved behavioral effect is also consistent with infantile amnesia affecting explicit memory more than implicit memory.
hint: Compare the person's internal condition during encoding and retrieval.

q: What is the reminiscence bump?
options: Enhanced recall for events from late adolescence and early adulthood
The near absence of adult episodic recall for events occurring before approximately age four
A sudden loss of memory for emotionally neutral adult events
Improved recall for events immediately before an interview
explain: The reminiscence bump is the elevated recall of events from roughly ages ten to thirty, especially late adolescence and early adulthood. It is distinct from infantile amnesia and immediate recency.
hint: Picture the lifespan recall curve and the period where it rises above baseline.

q: Which explanation says the reminiscence bump occurs because many remembered events happen while identity is being formed?
options: Cognitive hypothesis
Self-image hypothesis
State-dependent hypothesis
Misinformation hypothesis
explain: The self-image hypothesis links stronger memory to adolescence and young adulthood, when people assume identities and form a life story. The cognitive hypothesis instead emphasizes rapid change followed by stability.
hint: Which label directly names the construction of identity?

q: The cognitive hypothesis explains the reminiscence bump by proposing that encoding is especially strong during:
options: periods when daily experience is repetitive, predictable, and unchanged for many years
the years before language or a working self develops
rapid life change that is followed by a more stable period
any interval in which emotional arousal remains low
explain: The cognitive hypothesis emphasizes transitions: encoding is enhanced during rapid change, especially when a stable period follows. This differs from the self-image account's focus on identity formation.
hint: The chapter contrasts a period of transition with what comes after it.

q: A lifespan graph shows that people who emigrated at ages 34–35 have a later memory bump than people who emigrated at ages 20–24. Which conclusion does this pattern best support?
options: The bump is fixed biologically at ages ten to thirty and cannot move with a later transition
The bump occurs only when the hippocampus first matures
The bump depends entirely on whether an event was positive
A major life transition can shift the bump, as the cognitive hypothesis predicts
explain: The later bump for later emigrants shows that memorable periods can track the timing of rapid life change rather than a fixed age alone. That pattern supports the cognitive hypothesis.
hint: Ask what changed between the two groups besides their age at the remembered event.

q: Why are emotionally important events often remembered better than neutral events?
options: Emotion draws attention to the event and strengthens encoding
Emotion prevents all later reconstruction, distortion, or loss of the remembered event
Only negative events can enter autobiographical memory
Emotional events bypass the need for neural processing
explain: An emotional response draws attentional processing toward an event, increasing the likelihood of effective encoding and storage. Both positive and negative events can benefit, and emotion does not make memory fixed or bypass the brain.
hint: Trace the path from emotional importance to attention to storage.

q: In an attentional-blink task, what usually happens when two targets appear close together in a rapid stream?
options: The first target is missed because attention waits for and fully processes the second target instead
The second target is often missed after attention processes the first
Both targets enter permanent memory with equal accuracy
Only targets presented in pictures can be detected
explain: The attentional blink is the reduced detection of a second target appearing shortly after the first in a rapid sequence. The effect concerns temporary attentional processing, not guaranteed permanent storage or picture-only stimuli.
hint: Which of the two closely spaced targets suffers?

q: In Anderson's word-based attentional-blink study, participants saw 'chair' followed rapidly by either 'ball' or 'murder.' What result was reported?
options: The neutral word was detected more often because it required less arousal and fewer attentional resources
Both second words were missed equally often
The emotionally charged word was detected more often than the neutral word
Detection improved only when the first word was emotional
explain: Participants were more likely to detect the emotionally charged second word than the neutral second word. The result shows that emotional information can gain a processing advantage during the attentional blink.
hint: Which second word has greater emotional significance?

q: A researcher repeats the emotional attentional-blink task using threatening and ordinary words. Based on the chapter, what is the best prediction?
options: Ordinary words will always be recalled with perfect accuracy
Threatening words will be ignored because they consume attention
Both types will be processed identically once presentation is rapid
Threatening words will be more likely to break through the blink
explain: Emotionally charged words, especially negative ones, were more effectively processed and stored than neutral words. A threatening word should therefore be more likely to escape the usual attentional-blink deficit.
hint: Apply the emotional processing advantage to a new set of words.

q: Which structure is emphasized as supporting the enhanced encoding of emotionally valenced information?
options: Amygdala
Primary motor cortex
Cerebellum
Occipital pole
explain: The amygdala is activated by emotionally charged stimuli and is central to the chapter's account of emotional encoding. Damage to it reduces the emotional perceptual advantage.
hint: Choose the structure repeatedly linked to emotion in the deck.

q: What happened when patients with bilateral amygdala damage completed the emotional-blink paradigm?
options: They detected emotional targets better than healthy controls
They did not show the usual perceptual advantage for emotional words
They lost the ability to detect neutral words but not emotional words
They showed perfect recall for both emotional and neutral targets
explain: Bilateral amygdala damage eliminated the usual advantage for emotionally charged targets. This links the amygdala to the enhanced processing and encoding of emotional information.
hint: If the amygdala produces the advantage, predict what damage should remove.

q: Anderson and Phelps found that patients with damage limited to which side of the amygdala also lacked the emotional perceptual advantage?
options: Right side only
Either side produced an identical loss
Left side only
Neither side; only frontal damage mattered
explain: The slides report that patients with exclusively left, but not right, hemispheric amygdala damage failed to show the emotional perceptual advantage. This is more specific than the bilateral-damage result.
hint: The deck identifies one hemisphere, not both, for this word-based task.

q: Which event is most likely to produce a flashbulb memory as defined in the chapter?
options: A routine breakfast remembered only by one person after being rehearsed every morning for years
A practiced motor skill learned gradually over weeks
A neutral word repeated during a laboratory task
A highly emotional public event experienced by many people at once
explain: Flashbulb memories concern high-emotion events experienced by many people at the same time, such as a national tragedy. They feel vivid, but the chapter emphasizes that they are not permanent recordings.
hint: Look for both shared exposure and high emotion.

q: Neisser and Harsch compared people's accounts immediately after a large tragedy with accounts years later. What did they find?
options: Reports often changed or conflicted, although confidence remained high
Reports remained identical, proving that flashbulb memories do not decay
Participants forgot the public event but recalled neutral days perfectly
Confidence declined to zero even when reports stayed consistent
explain: Participants' later reports often differed from their initial accounts, yet they remained highly confident. The finding shows that vivid confidence does not guarantee flashbulb-memory accuracy.
hint: Separate how certain people felt from how consistent their reports were.

q: Talarico and Rubin tracked memories of September 11 and an everyday event. Which pattern distinguished the flashbulb memory?
options: Its accuracy and confidence both stayed perfect while everyday-memory accuracy and confidence fell
Its confidence stayed high even as its accuracy declined over time
Its confidence fell faster than confidence in the everyday memory
It lost accuracy only after participants discussed the event
explain: Accuracy declined for both flashbulb and everyday memories, although everyday memories declined more. Confidence stayed relatively stable for the flashbulb memory while confidence in the everyday memory declined more rapidly.
hint: Again, keep the accuracy curve separate from the confidence curve.

q: In the lesion comparison described by Davidson and colleagues, which group showed the weakest recall for the emotional public event?
options: Healthy controls with intact frontal and temporal lobes
Patients with frontal-lobe damage but an intact amygdala and preserved temporal-lobe function
Patients with temporal-lobe damage involving the amygdala
Participants selected for high frontal-lobe functioning
explain: Healthy controls and patients with frontal-lobe damage recalled the emotional event better than patients with temporal-lobe damage involving the amygdala. The comparison again supports an amygdala contribution to emotional memory.
hint: Which lesion group includes damage to the structure emphasized for emotion?

q: Brown and Kulik found that Black Americans were more likely than White Americans to report flashbulb memories for the deaths of Martin Luther King Jr. and Malcolm X. What principle does this illustrate?
options: Flashbulb memory depends only on how often news footage is repeated
A public event becomes a flashbulb memory for every observer equally
Cultural identity prevents any later change in a memory
Event relevance to identity can shape flashbulb-memory formation
explain: The cultural difference suggests that an event's personal and group relevance helps determine whether a flashbulb memory forms. Relevance improves memory without making it equally strong for everyone or immune to change.
hint: Ask why the same public events had different significance across groups.

q: What does it mean to say autobiographical memory is constructive?
options: Remembering is an interpretation assembled from experience and beliefs
Every event is stored as an unchanged sensory recording that later beliefs cannot influence
Only deliberately invented events count as autobiographical memories
Once encoded, a memory cannot be influenced by later information
explain: A constructive memory is an interpretation rather than a literal recording. Existing beliefs and later information can shape what is reconstructed, so sincere memories can contain errors.
hint: Contrast reconstruction with a camera-like recording.

q: Participants study 'bed, rest, pillow, slumber, awake' but never see 'sleep.' In the DRM paradigm, what commonly happens at recall?
options: Participants recall only the first and last presented words
About 40% report the unpresented critical lure 'sleep'
Participants reject every word related to the list's theme
The list is remembered only as an implicit motor response
explain: The slide labels the Deese-Roediger-McDermott paradigm 'RDM,' but its standard abbreviation is DRM. The related words activate the category theme, making the unpresented critical lure feel familiar; about 40% report 'sleep,' supporting semantically organized, constructive memory.
hint: The missing word names the theme connecting the entire list.

q: Why did Elizabeth Loftus question reports of newly recovered childhood abuse that appeared during suggestive therapy?
options: She argued that negative childhood events can never be remembered accurately after any delay
She believed autobiographical memory is always accurate under hypnosis
Therapists might inadvertently coach patients into constructing false memories
Patients cannot form semantic knowledge about childhood
explain: Loftus proposed that suggestive therapeutic techniques could unintentionally change memory and lead patients to construct events they sincerely believed were real. The slides do not claim that repressed memories are impossible, only that they are rare and suggestion is a serious concern.
hint: Focus on the effect of repeated suggestion from an authority figure.

q: Which statement defines the misinformation effect?
options: An old memory blocks the encoding of a new event and prevents it from entering long-term storage
A person intentionally lies about an accurately remembered event
Emotion makes a memory more vivid than an everyday event
Incorrect information introduced later becomes part of what is remembered
explain: The misinformation effect occurs when new, incorrect information is introduced after an event and becomes incorporated into memory. It is not deliberate deception or merely increased vividness.
hint: Track what happens when wrong information arrives after the original event.

q: In Loftus and Palmer's traffic-film study, how did the verb in the speed question affect estimates?
options: More forceful verbs produced higher estimated speeds
More forceful verbs produced lower estimated speeds
Verb choice changed confidence but never changed speed estimates
Only participants in the neutral control group estimated speed
explain: Participants watched seven crash videos presented in random order. They gave higher speed estimates when the question used a more forceful verb such as 'smashed' rather than milder wording such as 'contacted,' showing that wording shaped the reconstructed event.
hint: Match the implied force of the verb to the remembered speed.

q: In the follow-up traffic study, no broken glass appeared in the film. Which result best demonstrates the wording effect?
options: All three groups reported broken glass at the same high rate despite the different question wording
The 'smashed' group reported glass more often than the 'hit' and control groups
The control group reported glass more often than either verb group
Only the 'hit' group refused to answer the glass question
explain: Sixteen participants in the 'smashed' condition reported glass, compared with seven in the 'hit' condition and six in the control condition. The unpresented detail was therefore most often remembered after the strongest verb.
hint: The wording with the greatest implied force should produce the most false glass reports.

q: A witness sees two cars make minor contact. Later an interviewer repeatedly asks, 'How fast were they going when they smashed?' What is the deck-based prediction?
options: The witness will become immune to any later source confusion
The witness will necessarily know that the verb is misleading
The witness may remember a more severe crash than was shown
The wording can affect speed estimates but never event details
explain: Forceful wording can increase estimated speed and can also introduce details consistent with a severe crash, such as nonexistent broken glass. The effect demonstrates constructive memory, not guaranteed awareness of suggestion.
hint: Generalize both findings from the traffic-film studies.

q: What did Loftus and Pickrell's 'lost in public' study show?
options: People accept and richly elaborate every false childhood story supplied by relatives or researchers
False events can be created only through direct visual evidence
Participants remember real stories less accurately than neutral words
About one quarter accepted and elaborated an entirely false childhood event
explain: Participants received four childhood stories, one fabricated. About 25% accepted the false lost-in-public event and supplied details, showing that suggestion can generate an entirely new autobiographical memory.
hint: Recall the 25% versus 75% result on the slide.

q: How does the lost-in-public study extend the misinformation findings?
options: It shows suggestion can create a whole event, not just alter a detail
It proves that all suggested events are accepted as true and elaborated with detailed sensory information
It shows that false memories require an actual event to modify
It demonstrates that emotional memories cannot be reconstructed
explain: The traffic studies altered details of an event participants actually saw. The lost-in-public study showed that suggestion could produce acceptance and elaboration of an event that never occurred, although most participants still rejected it.
hint: Compare changing one feature with constructing the entire episode.

q: Why does false recall of the word 'sleep' in the DRM list support semantic organization of memory?
options: The word was secretly presented below conscious awareness
The shared meaning of the presented words activates their unpresented theme
Participants recall every possible category member once any related word from the category is shown
The lure is remembered only because it sounds like the other words
explain: Bed, rest, pillow, slumber, and awake share a semantic theme. That meaning-based organization can activate 'sleep' even though it was never presented, producing a convincing false memory.
hint: What links the list items: sound, order, or meaning?

q: Why can a false memory be described as a form of retroactive interference?
options: An earlier event prevents a later event from being encoded or retained in any form
A current goal selects one accurate memory over another
Later information interferes with a memory that was formed earlier
An emotional response blocks all information during encoding
explain: Retroactive interference runs backward in time: new information disrupts or alters access to older information. Misinformation and source confusion can therefore change what is remembered about the original event.
hint: Which direction does the interference travel: old to new, or new to old?

q: What is the core problem in a source-monitoring error?
options: The remembered item has no familiarity at all
The event was never encoded in any form
A person deliberately substitutes a false source
Familiar information is remembered but assigned to the wrong encounter
explain: Source monitoring requires remembering where or how information was encountered. A person may correctly recognize a familiar face or detail but misattribute that familiarity to the crime rather than a later video or other context.
hint: The content feels familiar; the mistake concerns where that familiarity came from.

q: A victim confidently identifies a man as an attacker, but evidence shows she had recently seen him on television. Which explanation best fits?
options: She recognized the face but misattributed the source of familiarity
She experienced infantile amnesia for the television program
She used the directive function to select the correct suspect
She converted an implicit motor skill into an episodic memory
explain: The victim may genuinely remember the man's face while confusing the television appearance with the assault. That is a source-monitoring error, not intentional deception or loss of all memory.
hint: Separate recognizing the person from remembering where he was seen.

q: In Ross and colleagues' classroom-video study, which participants were most likely to choose the innocent male observer as the robber?
options: Those who saw only the female teacher before the robbery clip
Those who saw the male observer with the teacher before the robbery clip
Those who completed no lineup after the robbery clip
Those who were warned that the observer was the robber
explain: Participants who had seen the male observer in the earlier classroom video tended to identify him in the later robbery lineup. Familiarity from the first video was misassigned to the robbery, demonstrating source confusion.
hint: Which group had an extra, innocent source of familiarity with a male face?

q: What is reconsolidation?
options: The permanent loss of every detail after retrieval
The initial encoding of a never-before-seen event
The restorage of an accessed memory, when it may be especially vulnerable
The conversion of episodic memory into an automatic skill
explain: Reconsolidation occurs when a stored memory is accessed and then stored again. During this period, the memory is vulnerable to modification by new information.
hint: Break the word into 'again' plus 'make stable.'

q: In Chan and colleagues' television-episode study, which group was more likely to remember later misinformation?
options: Participants who never watched the original episode
Participants who received no post-event questions
Participants who viewed the episode but were not asked to recall it
Participants who first retrieved and described the episode
explain: Participants who retrieved parts of the show before receiving misinformation were more likely to remember that misinformation. Retrieval reopened the memory, making it vulnerable during reconsolidation.
hint: Which group had the original memory actively reopened before the false detail arrived?

q: Why might reconsolidation be useful in therapy for post-traumatic stress disorder according to the chapter?
options: Retrieving a stored traumatic memory makes it temporarily vulnerable to change
Therapy can prove that the traumatic event never occurred
Reconsolidation permanently removes every autobiographical memory
The process prevents the memory from ever being retrieved again
explain: Retrieval makes a stored memory vulnerable during reconsolidation. The slides propose that using this process therapeutically may help people with PTSD find relief from ruminative and obsessive patterns; the goal is relief, not denial of the event or deletion of all memory.
hint: Use the vulnerable period to change the emotional response, not the historical fact.

q: In Schiller and colleagues' fear-learning study, what served as the conditioned cue?
options: The skin-conductance recording
A blue circle that had been paired with shock
The reminder given six hours after extinction
A verbal description of a traumatic event
explain: Participants repeatedly saw a blue circle followed by shock and developed a conditioned fear response to the circle. Skin conductance measured fear; it was not the cue itself.
hint: Choose the stimulus that came to predict the shock.

q: On the Schiller result slide, which condition is explicitly shown with no spontaneous recovery of fear when the blue circle was tested again?
options: No reminder before extinction
A reminder six hours before extinction
A reminder ten minutes before extinction
Shock exposure without any extinction trials
explain: A reminder ten minutes before extinction was followed by no recovered fear response, whereas the no-reminder condition showed fear when tested again. The result slide does not state the outcome of the six-hour condition.
hint: Which condition is shown on the result slide as eliminating recovered fear?

q: Why was the ten-minute reminder important in the Schiller study?
options: It made the blue circle more physically visible
It caused participants to forget that a shock had ever occurred
It measured fear without changing the stored association
It likely reopened the fear memory before extinction updated it
explain: The reminder likely reactivated the fear association, placing it into a vulnerable reconsolidation state. Extinction during that window could then update the response so fear did not spontaneously recover.
hint: Connect reminder, vulnerability, extinction, and restorage in that order.

q: Why is eyewitness testimony especially challenging for the criminal-justice system?
options: A witness can be highly confident in a memory that is inaccurate
Witnesses never retain any information about a stressful event
Juries automatically reject testimony delivered with confidence
Eyewitness memory is accurate unless a person intentionally lies
explain: Constructive memory, misinformation, and source errors can produce sincere but inaccurate testimony. Confidence can make that testimony persuasive even though confidence is not a guarantee of accuracy.
hint: Recall the repeated split between confidence and correctness.

q: What role of the Innocence Project is emphasized in the slides?
options: Training witnesses to increase confidence before trial
Using DNA evidence to exonerate people wrongly convicted after eyewitness testimony
Replacing all lineups with autobiographical interviews
Preventing investigators from collecting physical evidence
explain: The Innocence Project is described as lawyers using DNA evidence to overturn wrongful convictions based on eyewitness testimony. The slide reports 350 overturned cases since 1982 and estimates that 100,000–200,000 people have been wrongly convicted because of eyewitness testimony.
hint: The organization checks remembered identification against biological evidence.

q: Which interviewing practice best follows the chapter's recommendation for deposing an eyewitness?
options: Offer several possible stories and ask the witness to choose one
Begin with the investigator's theory so the account stays focused
Let the witness freely recall the event before introducing specific prompts
Repeat emotionally intense wording until the witness becomes certain
explain: The slides recommend free recall so investigators do not inject details into the account. Leading alternatives and emotionally loaded repetition increase the risk of misinformation.
hint: The safest opening gives the witness, not the officer, control of the narrative.

q: Why should witness statements use written, neutral language?
options: Written language guarantees that every remembered detail is true
Neutral wording makes witnesses forget the emotional event
Written statements prevent any need for later corroboration
Neutral wording reduces suggestion and emotional interference
explain: A written record preserves the statement, while neutral wording limits the interviewer's ability to reshape the memory through loaded language. It reduces risk; it does not guarantee accuracy.
hint: Connect this recommendation to Loftus and Palmer's wording effect.

q: Which lineup procedure is most consistent with the chapter?
options: Show one image at a time and warn that the assailant may be absent
Show all suspects together and imply that one must be guilty
Repeat the same favored suspect until the witness agrees
Tell the witness which person another observer selected
explain: The slide specifically recommends showing one image at a time and warning that the assailant may not be present. The other procedures add suggestion or pressure that can distort identification.
hint: Avoid forcing the witness to choose the closest-looking person in a group.

q: Which mapping matches the autobiographical-memory network described by Svoboda and colleagues?
options: PFC—motor habits; hippocampus—language; amygdala—visual acuity
PFC—multisensory reconstruction; hippocampus—retrieval; amygdala—emotional encoding and retrieval
PFC—fear conditioning only; hippocampus—muscle control; amygdala—semantic categories
PFC—hearing; hippocampus—reflexes; amygdala—numerical calculation
explain: The chapter describes a distributed network: especially left prefrontal cortex reconstructs multisensory information, the medial temporal lobe and hippocampus contribute to retrieval, and the amygdala contributes to emotional encoding and retrieval.
hint: Match executive reconstruction, long-term retrieval, and emotion to their familiar structures.

q: A patient can retrieve the outline of a personal event but has difficulty rebuilding its combined visual, spatial, and emotional context. Which region in the chapter's network is most directly associated with that reconstruction?
options: Primary auditory cortex
Cerebellar vermis
Prefrontal cortex, particularly the left side
Spinal reflex pathways
explain: The prefrontal cortex, especially the left lobe, is described as reconstructing the multisensory information of autobiographical memories. The chapter does not assign that integrative role to the other regions listed.
hint: Which part of the network coordinates and rebuilds several kinds of event information?

q: What artificial-intelligence connection appears in the chapter summary?
options: Artificial networks already possess human autobiographical memory
Semantic networks eliminate the need to learn from experience
Machines remember only by reproducing exact sensory recordings
Deep Q-learning with replay memory aims to use past experiences in later behavior
explain: The chapter notes that artificial neural networks do not have human long-term memory, but deep Q-learning and replay-memory methods are designed to let systems use stored past experiences. The deck provides this connection in the outline and summary rather than a full teaching section.
hint: Choose the option that treats remembered experience as input to later learning.

q: Davidson and colleagues used single-word cues immediately after September 11 and again one year later. What did the comparison of frontal-lobe functioning show?
options: People with both high and low frontal functioning could recall the emotional event
Only people with high frontal functioning retained any memory of the event
Low frontal functioning improved recall of neutral but not emotional material
Cues eliminated emotional-event recall in both frontal-function groups
explain: The slides report that people with both high and low frontal-lobe functioning could recall the emotionally valenced event. The result is consistent with emotion supporting recall even when frontal functioning varies.
hint: The study's notable result was similarity, not a total split, between the two frontal-function groups.

q: Why can flashbulb memories be better retained than ordinary events even though their details still change?
options: They are stored outside the normal autobiographical-memory system
Emotion strengthens encoding, and later thinking or talking promotes consolidation
They preserve exact detail because every observer encodes the same perspective
They avoid retrieval and therefore never enter reconsolidation
explain: A flashbulb event provokes an emotional response that supports encoding, and repeated thinking or discussion can promote consolidation. These advantages improve retention without making the memory an exact, permanent record.
hint: Combine what happens during the event with what people repeatedly do afterward.

q: In the self-memory system, what is meant by the goal of coherence?
options: Preserving every event-specific detail with equal strength
Retrieving memories in the exact order they were encoded
Supporting existing beliefs about oneself and the world
Separating autobiographical knowledge from current goals
explain: Coherence means that autobiographical remembering tends to support a person's existing beliefs about self and world. It does not require total detail, chronological recall, or separation from the working self.
hint: Think about consistency between a remembered past and the person's present worldview.

q: Which set of factors does the chapter identify as predicting which personal events are retained?
options: Motor speed, handedness, and visual acuity
Word length, rehearsal rate, and response time
Sleep stage, language family, and physical height
Emotion, importance, and the life stage when the event occurred
explain: The lifespan section highlights emotion, importance, and life stage as major predictors of autobiographical-memory retention. The remaining sets describe variables not presented as the chapter's three predictors.
hint: Choose the set that explains why milestones and meaningful transitions stand out.

q: After reading that Ayesha fell and her balloon floated away, most people infer that Ayesha is a child. Which memory system primarily supplies that inference?
options: Semantic memory
Iconic memory
The phonological loop
Procedural memory
explain: Semantic memory contains generalized knowledge about the world. Knowledge linking children with balloons supports the inference even though Ayesha's age was never stated.
hint: The answer depends on general world knowledge rather than a remembered personal episode.

q: A person immediately knows where Ayesha's nose is without ever having deliberately memorized that fact. The slides use this to illustrate that much knowledge is:
options: Stored only as a vivid episode
Implicit commonsense knowledge
Limited to verbal rules
Created anew without prior knowledge
explain: Commonsense knowledge can guide comprehension without having been consciously stated or studied. It is available implicitly and is easy for humans but difficult for computers.
hint: Ask whether the person must consciously recite a learned rule before answering.

q: In categorization research, a category is best defined as:
options: A single item remembered in detail and treated as the only member of a complete category
A temporary sensory trace
A set of items grouped by perceptual, biological, or functional familiarity
A feature that every object must possess
explain: A category is a set of related items. Categories organize the world by grouping things that are perceptually, biologically, or functionally familiar.
hint: The term refers to a group, not one member or one feature.

q: A particular robin that someone saw outside the library is an example of a(n):
options: Concept
Defining feature
Superordinate category
Exemplar
explain: An exemplar is an individual member of a category. The specific robin is one encountered instance of the broader category bird.
hint: Choose the term for one individual category member.

q: Which option best matches the slides' definition of a concept?
options: A mental representation of objects, ideas, or events
A list of every object ever encountered, preserved with complete sensory detail
A reflexive response to a stimulus
A brief visual afterimage
explain: A concept is the mental representation used to think about objects, ideas, or events. A category is the set of items; a concept is the representation of them.
hint: Separate the group in the world from its representation in the mind.

q: Under the classical view, a set of features defines a category only when those features are:
options: Typical and frequently encountered by every person in the same culture
Necessary and sufficient for membership
Learned through direct reinforcement
Stored as multiple exemplars
explain: The classical view treats categories as sharply defined. Necessary features must be present, and a sufficient set guarantees membership.
hint: The classical approach assumes rule-like boundaries rather than fuzzy similarity.

q: A three-legged dog that cannot bark is still readily recognized as a dog. This case most directly challenges which classical-view claim?
options: Knowledge is interconnected and always available to conscious inspection
Semantic memory supports inference
Most natural categories have a fixed set of defining features
Individual category members are exemplars
explain: If four legs and barking were necessary defining features, the animal could not be a dog. Natural categories often lack a clean feature set that is both necessary and sufficient.
hint: Ask what happens when a familiar member lacks supposedly required features.

q: Understanding the Ayesha-and-balloon sentence requires knowledge about children, balloons, bodies, and gravity to work together. This best demonstrates that knowledge is:
options: Entirely episodic
Stored as isolated facts
Only consciously accessible
Interconnected
explain: Multiple related knowledge structures cooperate to support comprehension and inference. The example illustrates a connected knowledge system rather than isolated facts.
hint: Several different kinds of prior knowledge contribute to one interpretation.

q: Which description best distinguishes semantic memory from a vivid episodic memory?
options: Semantic memory retains generalized information after specific sensory details are lost
Semantic memory always includes the original place, time, emotion, and every sensory detail from learning
Semantic memory lasts only a few seconds
Semantic memory is limited to motor skills
explain: Semantic memory preserves generalized knowledge without the full sensory and contextual details of a specific episode. That knowledge can then guide new inferences.
hint: Think generalized fact versus personally relived event.

q: A robin is judged to be a better example of a bird than an ostrich. This difference is called a:
options: Source-monitoring error
Typicality effect
Property-inheritance error
Serial-position effect
explain: Typicality effects occur when some category members are treated as better representatives than others, even though both are legitimate members.
hint: The judgment concerns how representative each member seems.

q: What did Rosch's 1975 typicality-rating procedure ask participants to do?
options: Memorize every category definition word for word before producing a rating
Name every object they had seen
Rate how good an example each item was of a category
Decide whether two items had identical features
explain: Participants rated the goodness of category examples. Their high agreement showed that typicality judgments are systematic rather than random personal preferences.
hint: The dependent measure was a judgment of category representativeness.

q: Which result is a standard behavioral signature of a typicality effect?
options: Atypical members are always rejected from the category
All category members are named equally quickly
Typical members require more conscious effort
Typical members are named first and categorized faster
explain: People tend to produce typical examples first and classify them faster than atypical examples. Membership may be equal, but processing is not.
hint: Typical examples enjoy a processing advantage.

q: The finding that 12-month-olds respond more readily to a robin than to an ostrich was used to argue that:
options: Typicality effects appear early in development
Infants use only classical definitions based on necessary and sufficient features
Atypical birds are not category members
Semantic knowledge begins in adulthood
explain: The slides use this finding to show that differential treatment of typical and atypical members begins early in life.
hint: Focus on what the participants' age implies.

q: In a lexical decision task, a participant must decide whether:
options: An object belongs to a biological category
A letter string is a real word
A sentence is autobiographically accurate
Two pictures were shown in the same location
explain: Lexical decision tasks present letter strings and ask whether each is a word. Reaction time can reveal semantic priming.
hint: Lexical refers to words.

q: A participant identifies a target word faster after first seeing a semantically related word. This pattern is an example of:
options: Cognitive economy
Property inheritance
Lexical priming
Repeated reproduction
explain: A semantically related prime speeds identification of a target word. The effect reflects activation spreading through related knowledge.
hint: The first word facilitates a related second word.

q: According to the slides, semantic priming should generally be strongest when the target word is:
options: A nonword
Unrelated to the prime
An atypical category member
A typical category member
explain: Priming effects are more robust for typical words because they are strongly connected to the category and related concepts.
hint: Choose the member with the strongest category relationship.

q: In Onishi et al. (2008), people repeated a sentence containing both 'turkey' and 'robin.' Which word was more likely to survive accurately in reproduction?
options: Robin, because it is the more typical bird
Turkey, because atypical items always dominate memory
Both were necessarily recalled equally
Neither, because typicality affects only perception
explain: Participants were more likely to reproduce the typical word. The study shows that object typicality influences language and memory, not just category ratings.
hint: The more representative category member had the recall advantage.

q: Why are typicality effects difficult for a strict classical theory of categorization to explain?
options: The classical theory predicts no item can ever belong to a natural category without a stored exemplar
Necessary-and-sufficient membership rules do not predict graded goodness among members
The classical theory stores too many exemplars
Typicality occurs only for artificial categories
explain: A strict rule says an item either satisfies the definition or it does not. It provides no natural reason for one valid member to seem 'more' representative than another.
hint: Compare an all-or-none boundary with a graded judgment.

q: Prototype theory replaces strict defining features with:
options: A hierarchy that contains no similarity information between any of its category levels
A record of one recent episode
Characteristic features that are probable but not required
An essence that guarantees membership
explain: Prototype theory is probabilistic. It represents features that are characteristic of category members even though no single feature is required in every case.
hint: The theory uses likely features rather than mandatory ones.

q: Which statement about a prototype is correct?
options: It is a complete record of every exemplar stored separately with all original sensory details
It must be the first member learned
It contains only necessary features
It is an average representation that may not correspond to any real item
explain: A prototype combines characteristic features into an abstract category average. No actual encountered member has to match it perfectly.
hint: It is a summary representation, not necessarily a real object.

q: A robin is a prototypical bird in the United States, whereas a rainbow lorikeet may be prototypical in Australia. This demonstrates that prototypes:
options: Depend on context and experience
Are fixed by biological necessity
Contain every stored exemplar
Eliminate typicality effects
explain: The most characteristic category representation depends on the examples common in a person's environment and culture.
hint: The same category produces different best examples in different environments.

q: Why is a penguin a challenge for a simple bird prototype based on flying, nesting, and living on land?
options: Penguins have no genuine category membership because flight is required of every bird
It is a valid but atypical member that poorly matches the prototype
Prototype theory requires all members to be identical
Penguins can only be represented as superordinate categories
explain: The penguin belongs to the bird category despite lacking many prototypical features. Prototype similarity is useful but cannot alone make membership perfectly clear.
hint: It belongs even though its feature match is weak.

q: Exemplar theory proposes that a new object is categorized by comparing it with:
options: A complete list of necessary features that must appear in every category member
A single abstract average only
Actual category members stored from past experience
A biological essence that cannot be observed
explain: Exemplar theory retains experienced category members. A new item is classified according to its similarity to those stored examples.
hint: This theory keeps concrete examples rather than only an average.

q: In Dopkins and Gleason's rectangle task, an ambiguous rectangle matched Category 2's prototype but was even more similar to one stored Category 1 item. Participants usually chose Category 1. Which theory did that result favor?
options: Classical feature theory
Psychological essentialism
Prototype theory
Exemplar theory
explain: Choosing the category containing the closest previously seen rectangle supports comparison with stored exemplars over comparison with only a category prototype.
hint: The deciding evidence was similarity to one remembered item.

q: Armstrong, Gleitman, and Gleitman found that people gave typicality ratings even to clearly rule-defined categories such as odd numbers. What does this finding show?
options: Graded typicality judgments can occur even when membership is all-or-none
Odd numbers do not follow a membership rule
Prototype theory completely explains every categorization process, including formal rules and social categories
Exemplars are never used for natural categories
explain: People can judge some rule-defined members as better examples despite every member satisfying the same formal rule. Typicality and membership are not the same judgment.
hint: Separate whether an item belongs from how representative it feels.

q: Murphy and Medin noted that a banana and a television share infinitely many trivial similarities, such as both being sold in stores. What key problem does this create for pure similarity theories?
options: Categories cannot contain visual information unless every exemplar shares the same appearance
The theories do not specify which similarities should matter
Only prototypes can be consciously recalled
All similarities are equally diagnostic
explain: Prototype and exemplar accounts need a way to select relevant comparison features. Broad knowledge and theories help determine which similarities are meaningful.
hint: Similarity alone needs a rule for feature relevance.

q: In the bird-picture naming graph, experts favored specific labels such as 'sparrow' or 'robin,' whereas nonexperts favored the basic label 'bird.' What does this show?
options: The basic level is preferred equally by experts and nonexperts in every categorization task
Experts lose access to basic categories
Expertise can shift naming from the basic level toward subordinate labels
Nonexperts rely mainly on superordinate labels
explain: The visual figure shows that the basic level is the common default for nonexperts, but specialized knowledge makes more specific subordinate labels useful and accessible to experts.
hint: Compare which naming level each knowledge group used most often.

q: Knowledge-based theories of categorization argue that people decide category membership by relying primarily on:
options: A long-lasting sensory persistence that retains every perceptual detail from the object
A single average shape
Random feature counts
Broad background knowledge about how features fit together
explain: Knowledge-based accounts use a person's wider theories and knowledge to explain why features cohere and which features matter.
hint: The account goes beyond surface similarity.

q: Psychological essentialism is the belief that:
options: Category members share an underlying essence that determines membership
All categories are stored as complete visual prototypes without semantic or functional knowledge
Only explicit rules can produce categories
Category boundaries never influence social judgment
explain: Essentialism assumes a deep, category-specific essence causes membership, even when that essence is not directly observable.
hint: Think hidden underlying nature rather than a checklist of visible features.

q: In Murphy and Allopenna's building study, which description was learned more easily?
options: Purple desert building occupied by farmers and reached by submarine
Red underwater building occupied by divers and reached by submarine
A list of randomly selected building features
Both descriptions were learned equally because they had equal length
explain: The underwater/diver/submarine features formed a coherent knowledge-based structure. Existing knowledge made that combination easier to learn than an incoherent set.
hint: Choose the description whose features make causal and thematic sense together.

q: Why does a coherent underwater-building description support knowledge-based categorization more than a pure feature-list account?
options: It contains fewer words
It matches one stored rectangle
Existing knowledge explains why its features belong together
It supplies necessary and sufficient features for all buildings
explain: Background knowledge links divers, underwater locations, thick windows, and submarines. The relations among features improve learning beyond raw similarity or list length.
hint: The advantage comes from meaningful relations among features.

q: What is the central risk of applying essentialist thinking to social categories?
options: It prevents all categorization of people while leaving biological categorization unchanged
It makes biological categories impossible
It eliminates cognitive efficiency
It encourages stereotyping by treating group members as sharing fixed qualities
explain: Assuming a fixed group essence can turn a useful organizing tendency into incorrect generalizations about people. Membership does not mean every person shares the same characteristics.
hint: The danger is overgeneralizing from group membership to personal traits.

q: Which question resembles an item from Bastian and Haslam's measure of essentialist beliefs?
options: Do people belong to discrete groups with biologically based qualities?
How many unrelated letters fit in short-term memory after rehearsal is prevented?
Does a tone cue one row of an iconic display?
How quickly can a participant count backward by threes?
explain: Their questions probed whether groups are discrete, personal qualities are changeable, and group membership has a biological basis.
hint: Choose the item about fixed group nature.

q: Bastian and Haslam found that stronger essentialist beliefs were associated with more:
options: Accurate sensory memory
Stereotyping
Visual working-memory capacity
Episodic detail
explain: Treating social groups as discrete and biologically fixed was linked with stereotyping. The association illustrates a cost of essentialist categorization.
hint: Think of the social consequence emphasized in the slides.

q: Why does the mind categorize people and objects despite the risk of mistaken assumptions?
options: Categorization increases the duration and precision of every sensory memory trace
It prevents all uncertainty
It reduces cognitive load in a complex world
It stores every experience verbatim
explain: Categorization compresses complexity and supports quick inference. The efficiency is useful, but it can also produce overgeneralization and stereotypes.
hint: The benefit is mental efficiency.

q: When comparing an apple with a banana, a person emphasizes edibility and fruit status but ignores that both an apple and a television can be sold in stores. A knowledge-based account explains this by saying that:
options: All shared features have equal weight, no matter how trivial or unrelated to the category
Only visual similarity matters
A prototype automatically deletes weak matches
Background knowledge selects features that are meaningful for the category
explain: Knowledge about fruits makes edibility and biological origin relevant, while store availability is incidental. Broader theories guide feature selection.
hint: The issue is how the mind decides which similarities matter.

q: Rosch described the basic level of a category hierarchy as 'just right' because it is:
options: Both informative and distinctive
Maximally general and minimally informative
A single individual exemplar
Defined only by an invisible essence
explain: Basic-level categories balance useful information with distinction from other groups. They are often the natural level used in everyday naming.
hint: It balances what an item is like with how it differs from others.

q: A subordinate category is generally:
options: Distinctive but not informative because it gives no useful detail about any category member
Informative but less distinctive because it shares many features with nearby members
Neither informative nor specific
The highest level in a hierarchy
explain: Subordinate categories provide detailed information, but their members share many properties with other members of the same basic category, reducing distinctiveness.
hint: It is a narrow, detailed level below the basic category.

q: A superordinate category is best characterized as:
options: Highly detailed and narrowly specific
An individual stored example
Distinctive but relatively uninformative
A category with no relation to basic levels
explain: A superordinate category separates a broad class from other classes but tells relatively little about the detailed properties of a member.
hint: It is broad enough to distinguish a class but sacrifices detail.

q: In Collins and Quillian's hierarchical network, concepts are represented as:
options: Motor programs
Sensory images only
Unconnected verbal lists with a separate property copied beside every category member
Nodes linked to properties and other nodes
explain: The model stores concepts in nodes. Connections represent category relations and properties, forming a hierarchical network.
hint: Picture concepts as points connected in a network.

q: What does cognitive economy mean in the Collins–Quillian model?
options: A property is stored at the highest appropriate level instead of repeated at every lower node
Every exemplar stores a complete duplicate of all category properties at every network level, whether shared or unique
Only frequently used concepts are retained
Reaction time is ignored to conserve processing
explain: To limit storage, a shared property such as 'has skin' is stored once at a higher node and accessed by connected lower categories.
hint: The model avoids duplicating the same fact at many nodes.

q: If 'bird' is linked to the property 'has feathers,' how does the model conclude that a canary has feathers?
options: Prototype averaging
Property inheritance
Repeated reproduction
Source monitoring
explain: A subordinate node inherits properties stored at connected superordinate nodes. Canary inherits the properties assigned to bird.
hint: The lower category receives a property from a higher category.

q: The hierarchical model predicts that verifying 'A canary is an animal' should be slower than verifying 'A canary is a bird' because:
options: Animal is a more typical word and therefore always takes longer to recognize than bird
Canaries are not true animals
The first judgment requires traversing more hierarchical links
Bird properties are stored in working memory
explain: Activation must travel farther through the hierarchy to reach animal than bird. Greater network distance predicts longer sentence-verification time.
hint: Count the number of levels between the starting and target nodes.

q: Participants verified 'A pig is an animal' faster than 'A pig is a mammal,' contrary to simple hierarchical distance. This result is a criticism because it shows that:
options: People cannot verify any true or false category statement once more than one level is involved
Property inheritance never occurs
Semantic priming is impossible
Reaction time does not always increase with the number of hierarchical levels
explain: The result reverses the model's distance prediction. It shows that the strict hierarchical link-count rule is not sufficient to explain every verification time.
hint: Compare the observed reaction times with the link-distance rule.

q: How does Collins and Loftus's spreading-activation model differ from the earlier Collins–Quillian hierarchy?
options: Concepts are connected by semantic relatedness rather than fixed hierarchical levels
It stores no connections between concepts
It rejects lexical priming evidence
Every concept must have a fixed set of necessary and sufficient features stored at one hierarchical level
explain: The spreading-activation model removes the strict hierarchy. Activation travels among concepts linked by semantic similarity, helping explain priming and typicality.
hint: The later network is organized by closeness in meaning.

q: A schema is:
options: A single sensory receptor that activates whenever any member of the topic appears
An organized knowledge base about a topic
A necessary feature of every category
A temporary virtual lesion
explain: Schemas group related information into organized knowledge structures. They guide interpretation and reconstruction.
hint: Think organized topic knowledge.

q: What was Bartlett's method of repeated reproduction designed to reveal?
options: The maximum capacity of visual working memory across several repeated reproduction trials
Whether a prototype must physically exist
How a remembered image changes across repeated reproductions over time
How TMS alters semantic decisions
explain: Participants repeatedly recreated an image from memory across days. Changes showed how prior knowledge and schemas shape reconstruction.
hint: The same material was reproduced from memory on successive occasions.

q: Across Bartlett's repeated reproductions, details were lost but the drawing increasingly resembled a familiar face. The best interpretation is that memory is:
options: A literal recording that improves with time
Independent of past knowledge
Stored only in the anterior temporal lobe
Reconstructive and guided by schemas
explain: When exact detail faded, participants used schematic knowledge of faces to rebuild the image. The result demonstrates reconstruction, not verbatim replay.
hint: Prior knowledge fills gaps left by lost details.

q: Dijkstra and Post found that memory for a past dental visit improved when participants were reclined as they had been during the visit. This supports the claim that:
options: Matching body position can aid autobiographical retrieval
Only verbal retrieval cues support memory because body position cannot affect cognition
Schemas eliminate state effects
Semantic memory is stored in muscles
explain: Retrieval improved when body position matched the original experience. Embodied cognition proposes that bodily state can influence cognitive access.
hint: The bodily context at retrieval matched encoding.

q: People who held a hot coffee rated a described person as warmer than people who held iced coffee. The slides use this finding as evidence that:
options: Temperature changes category membership rules
Bodily sensation can influence an abstract social judgment
Hot objects erase semantic knowledge
Lexical decisions require motor cortex
explain: A physical warmth experience influenced a conceptual judgment of interpersonal warmth, consistent with embodied or grounded cognition.
hint: A concrete bodily state shaped an abstract evaluation.

q: Hauk, Johnsrude, and Pulvermüller found related neural activity when people moved the tongue, fingers, or feet and when they silently read 'lick,' 'pick,' or 'kick.' What does this suggest?
options: Word meaning is independent of sensorimotor systems
Only actual movement activates motor areas
Thinking about actions recruits systems related to performing them
Semantic dementia improves motor language
explain: Action-word meaning was associated with activity in corresponding motor regions. This supports grounded representations tied to bodily systems.
hint: Compare action execution with action-word comprehension.

q: The central proposal of embodied cognition is that:
options: Knowledge is stored only as abstract symbols
The body has no effect on thought
All cognition is conscious
Bodily states and systems influence cognition
explain: Embodied accounts argue that cognition is shaped by bodily position, sensation, and sensorimotor systems rather than being fully detached from them.
hint: The theory connects thinking with bodily experience.

q: The speaker note reports that judges granted parole about 65% of the time just after meal breaks but nearly 0% just before them. Which interpretation matches the chapter's use of this example?
options: Bodily states such as hunger or fatigue can influence judgment
Meal breaks alter the legal definition of parole
Judges use necessary and sufficient features only after eating
Parole decisions are stored as autobiographical episodes
explain: The sharp shift around meals is presented as evidence that bodily condition can influence a complex decision, consistent with embodied cognition.
hint: Connect the timing of a physical need with the change in judgment.

q: According to the chapter summary, a limitation of purely symbolic artificial neural-network knowledge is that the symbols:
options: Can produce useful output only when every category has a necessary-and-sufficient rule plus a complete prototype
Are not grounded in direct real-world experience unless connected through systems such as robotics
Always operate as classical categories
Are stored only in short-term memory
explain: Assigned symbols can support relevant outputs, but without sensors and action they lack the direct experiential grounding available to embodied human cognition.
hint: Contrast assigned computer symbols with concepts tied to perception and action.

q: Semantic dementia is characterized by a progressive loss of:
options: Only visual acuity
Short-term digit span
Semantic knowledge
All motor reflexes
explain: Patients progressively lose knowledge of object names and characteristics even though basic perception, language mechanics, and intelligence may initially remain relatively intact.
hint: The lost information concerns meanings and facts.

q: Semantic dementia is most strongly associated in the slides with degeneration of the:
options: Primary visual cortex
Dorsolateral prefrontal cortex
Cerebellum
Anterior temporal lobe
explain: Anterior temporal lobe degeneration is strongly associated with semantic dementia, motivating hub-based theories of semantic representation.
hint: The region is abbreviated ATL.

q: Why did healthy-brain neuroimaging initially seem inconsistent with a simple anterior-temporal-lobe account of semantic knowledge?
options: Semantic tasks often showed widely distributed activity rather than ATL activation alone
Healthy people had no semantic knowledge and therefore showed no cortical activity during any meaning task
The ATL responded only during working-memory delay periods
Neuroimaging showed activity exclusively in the spinal cord
explain: Patient evidence pointed to the ATL, while healthy semantic tasks recruited distributed cortex. The hub-and-spoke model was proposed to reconcile these findings.
hint: Compare localized lesion evidence with distributed imaging activity.

q: The hub-and-spoke model proposes that semantic knowledge is represented by:
options: One sensory trace in the ATL with no interaction with any distributed cortical feature system
An anterior-temporal hub interacting with distributed modality-specific spokes
A fixed hierarchy confined to the frontal lobe
Only motor programs for category names
explain: The model combines an ATL hub for generalized meaning with distributed spokes that represent modality- and context-specific details.
hint: The model deliberately combines localized and distributed storage.

q: Within the hub-and-spoke model, the anterior temporal hub primarily represents:
options: Raw retinal input
Temporary phonological rehearsal
Generalized and abstract semantic knowledge
Only autobiographical episodes
explain: The hub integrates information across modalities into abstract, generalized concepts that are not tied to one sensory channel.
hint: The hub supplies the cross-modal core meaning.

q: In the hub-and-spoke model, the cortical spokes store:
options: Only category labels
One central abstract essence
No semantic content
Context-dependent and modality-specific details
explain: Spokes distributed across cortex represent features tied to vision, action, sound, and other modalities. The hub integrates those details.
hint: Spokes preserve the feature-specific parts of a concept.

q: Why is transcranial magnetic stimulation useful for studying semantic representation?
options: It can temporarily disrupt a localized cortical region, creating a virtual lesion
It permanently removes the anterior temporal lobe and every connected semantic region during one trial
It directly reads every stored concept
It measures only peripheral nerve conduction
explain: TMS noninvasively stimulates a localized cortical area and can temporarily impair its function. This allows causal tests without permanent injury.
hint: Its key advantage is a temporary, localized disruption.

q: Pobric, Jefferies, and Ralph applied TMS to the anterior temporal lobe and inferior parietal lobule. Their findings were used to support which conclusion?
options: Knowledge resides in one isolated neuron
Knowledge has both localized and distributed neural components
The ATL has no role in meaning
Semantic representations are entirely symbolic and nonneural
explain: The TMS evidence fits a system with specialized local contributions embedded in a wider distributed network, consistent with hub-and-spoke organization.
hint: The result reconciles local-region and whole-network accounts.

q: A patient can see a pictured object clearly and speak fluently but cannot name it or describe its usual properties. Which interpretation best fits Warrington's semantic-memory cases?
options: The deficit must be a failure of sensory encoding because intact perception cannot coexist with loss of semantic meaning
The patient has only a motor-speech problem
Semantic knowledge can be selectively impaired despite relatively preserved perception and language
The patient has unusually strong prototype knowledge
explain: Warrington's patients showed loss of object meaning and characteristics with other abilities relatively intact. This is evidence that semantic knowledge is a distinguishable cognitive and neural function.
hint: The object is perceived, but its meaning is unavailable.

q: The visual evidence figure plots sentence-verification time against the number of hierarchy levels traversed for both category and property statements. Which pattern supports the original semantic-network model?
options: Reaction time falls to zero as more hierarchical links are crossed for both category and property judgments
Property statements are always instant regardless of distance
Category statements cannot be verified at any level
Mean response time generally rises as the judgment requires more hierarchical links
explain: In the visual figure, verification becomes slower as a judgment crosses more levels for both category and property claims. That general distance effect supports the model, although later counterexamples show it is not a complete account.
hint: Follow the overall direction of the lines as levels-to-be-traversed increases.
